# Ground Truth: Open SaaS Template

## Metadata
- **Date audited:** 2026-03-16
- **Audited by:** Auto-generated auditor
- **Skill focus:** Privacy by Design review against Ann Cavoukian's 7 principles

## Must-Find Items

Critical findings any competent assessment should identify within this skill's focus area.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning | Acceptable Alternatives |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | No cascading delete constraints on User relations in schema.prisma - deleting a user does not automatically delete their GptResponse, Task, File, or ContactFormMessage records, potentially leaving orphaned PII | Data Minimization / Privacy by Default | HIGH | HIGH | Lines 25-28, 36-37, 46-47, 58-59, 105-106 in schema.prisma show relations without explicit onDelete: Cascade. Orphaned data violates data minimization and creates retention/GDPR compliance risks | Could argue LOW severity if documented deletion procedures exist, but none found in codebase |
| 2 | User-generated content stored unencrypted in database - GptResponse.content (line 39), Task.description (line 49), ContactFormMessage.content (line 108) in schema.prisma contain potentially sensitive user data with no encryption at rest | Proactive not Reactive / End-to-End Security | CRITICAL | HIGH | These fields store user prompts, task descriptions, and messages which may contain PII, health info, financial data, etc. No encryption layer found in operations files or schema | Some might argue this is database-layer responsibility, but application-layer encryption is a PbD best practice |
| 3 | AWS S3 credentials stored in environment variables (AWS_S3_IAM_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_S3_IAM_SECRET_KEY) in .env.server.example lines 60-61, accessed directly in src/file-upload/s3Utils.ts lines 16-20 without rotation mechanism | End-to-End Security | HIGH | HIGH | Long-lived credentials without rotation increase breach risk. No evidence of secret rotation, credential management service, or least-privilege IAM role usage | Could use IAM roles instead of access keys for better security posture |
| 4 | Admin emails hardcoded in plaintext environment variable (ADMIN_EMAILS) in .env.server.example line 33, used in src/auth/userSignupFields.ts lines 5-7 to auto-grant admin privileges | Privacy by Default / Minimize Collection | MEDIUM | HIGH | Storing admin email list in env vars creates privacy risk if env file is compromised. Auto-granting admin based on email bypasses proper authorization mechanisms | Could argue this is acceptable for initial setup, but production should use database-backed RBAC |
| 5 | No data retention policy - User.createdAt, User.datePaid, GptResponse.createdAt/updatedAt, Task.createdAt, File.createdAt, ContactFormMessage.createdAt in schema.prisma have no associated TTL or cleanup jobs | Data Minimization | HIGH | HIGH | Data persists indefinitely without cleanup. No scheduled job for purging old data found (only dailyStatsJob exists for analytics) | Could argue retention policy is business decision, but PbD requires proactive minimization |
| 6 | Payment processor user IDs (User.paymentProcessorUserId line 18) exposed in admin UI (src/admin/dashboards/users/UsersTable.tsx line 303-306) with label "Stripe ID" | Minimize Collection / Visibility and Transparency | MEDIUM | HIGH | Exposing third-party identifiers to all admins violates need-to-know principle. These IDs could be used to cross-reference users in Stripe/LemonSqueezy/Polar systems | Some might view this as necessary for admin support, but access should be logged/restricted |
| 7 | Console logging of potentially sensitive data - multiple console.log/error statements throughout codebase (31 occurrences found) including in payment webhooks (stripe/webhook.ts, lemonSqueezy/webhook.ts), analytics stats.ts, and operations | Proactive not Reactive / Data Minimization | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Console logs may expose PII in production logs. Examples: src/analytics/stats.ts line 119 logs dailyStats, payment webhooks log customer data on errors | Could argue INFO level logging is acceptable with proper log management, but best practice is structured logging with PII redaction |
| 8 | No user data export functionality - no API endpoint or UI for users to download their data (required for GDPR Article 15 data portability) | Respect for User Privacy / Full Functionality | HIGH | HIGH | Searched for "export" patterns in user operations - none found. Users cannot exercise data portability rights | Clear violation of PbD principle of respecting user privacy through transparency |
| 9 | No user account deletion functionality - no self-service delete option in AccountPage.tsx or user/operations.ts | Respect for User Privacy / Full Functionality | CRITICAL | HIGH | Only admin can modify users (updateIsUserAdminById in operations.ts lines 19-46). No user-initiated deletion found. GDPR Article 17 "right to be forgotten" not implemented | This is a fundamental privacy right - must be implemented |
| 10 | Third-party data sharing with no granular consent - Google Analytics, Plausible, Stripe, LemonSqueezy, Polar, OpenAI integrations active by default in config, only cookie consent banner for analytics (src/client/components/cookie-consent/Config.ts) | Respect for User Privacy / Visibility and Transparency | HIGH | HIGH | Payment and AI providers receive user data automatically without explicit per-service consent. Cookie consent only covers analytics, not payment/AI data flows | Industry norm is bundled consent for essential services, but PbD requires granular control |
| 11 | OpenAI API receives user task descriptions (src/demo-ai-app/operations.ts lines 247-264) with no data processing agreement disclosure or opt-out | Visibility and Transparency / Respect for User Privacy | HIGH | HIGH | Tasks sent to OpenAI (line 262) may contain sensitive information. No privacy notice about third-party AI processing found in UI | Users should be informed their data leaves the application boundary |
| 12 | Email addresses stored in localStorage by cookie consent mechanism (vanilla-cookieconsent) and useLocalStorage hook (src/client/hooks/useLocalStorage.ts) with no expiry or encryption | End-to-End Security / Privacy by Default | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | localStorage persists indefinitely and is accessible to all JS on the domain. Used for preferences (line 18-37) but could leak if XSS vulnerability exists | Could argue this is low-risk preference data, but PbD principle is minimize client-side storage |
| 13 | No privacy policy or terms of service links - cookie consent Config.ts lines 101-103 has placeholder URLs "<your-url-here>" for privacy policy and T&C | Visibility and Transparency | HIGH | HIGH | Users cannot review privacy practices before consent. Template ships without privacy documentation | This is a template so acceptable as TODO, but must be flagged as blocker for production |
| 14 | Database connection string in environment variable (DATABASE_URL) with no connection encryption settings specified in schema.prisma line 3 | End-to-End Security | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Prisma defaults to unencrypted connections for some providers. No explicit SSL/TLS enforcement in datasource config | Could argue Prisma handles this automatically for cloud providers, but PbD requires explicit security declarations |
| 15 | Session/authentication data handled by Wasp framework - no visible session management, timeout, or secure cookie configuration in codebase | End-to-End Security / Privacy by Default | MEDIUM | LOW | Wasp abstracts auth (main.wasp lines 34-72) but no explicit session timeout, cookie security flags (httpOnly, secure, sameSite) visible in app code | Framework may handle this correctly, but audit cannot verify without Wasp internals |

## Nice-to-Find Items

Deeper findings that demonstrate thoroughness.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | No rate limiting on sensitive operations - generateGptResponse, createTask, file upload operations have no visible throttling | Proactive not Reactive | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Could enable data exfiltration or resource abuse. Best practice is rate limiting on all user-facing mutations |
| 2 | Username field is optional (schema.prisma line 15) but displayed in admin UI and AccountPage - inconsistent handling could cause UX issues or data leakage | Positive-Sum / Full Functionality | LOW | MEDIUM | If username is null, admin sees undefined/blank. Better to have consistent display logic |
| 3 | File.type and File.name (schema.prisma lines 61-62) store original user-provided values - could contain sensitive information in filenames | Data Minimization | LOW | MEDIUM | Original filenames might reveal private information (e.g., "medical-diagnosis-2024.pdf"). S3 uses randomized keys (s3Utils.ts line 88) but metadata persists |
| 4 | Analytics tracking starts before cookie consent in some cases - Plausible script tags in main.wasp lines 29-30 use defer but load on page render | Respect for User Privacy | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Script loads even if user hasn't consented yet, though Plausible claims no cookies. Best practice is conditional loading post-consent |
| 5 | LemonSqueezy customer portal URL stored in database (User.lemonSqueezyCustomerPortalUrl line 19) - URL might contain sensitive tokens | End-to-End Security | LOW | MEDIUM | Portal URLs may have embedded auth tokens. If DB is compromised, attacker could access customer portals |
| 6 | Logs table (schema.prisma lines 93-99) stores arbitrary messages with no PII filtering - error logs could inadvertently capture sensitive data | Data Minimization | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Generic message field could capture stack traces, user IDs, emails from error contexts |
| 7 | DailyStats and PageViewSource (schema.prisma lines 66-91) aggregate user behavior data indefinitely - no anonymization or aggregation threshold | Data Minimization | LOW | MEDIUM | While aggregated, fine-grained daily stats persist forever. Best practice is rolling aggregation with deletion |
| 8 | Email verification and password reset links contain no visible expiry in emails.ts - tokens might be long-lived | End-to-End Security | MEDIUM | LOW | Email templates (src/auth/email-and-pass/emails.ts) don't show token expiry. Wasp framework likely handles this, but not visible |
| 9 | No Content Security Policy headers visible - XSS could enable data exfiltration from localStorage | End-to-End Security | MEDIUM | LOW | No CSP configuration found in wasp config or middleware. Modern security best practice |
| 10 | Mock user generation in dbSeeds.ts (lines 16-59) creates fake user data - if accidentally run in production, creates data integrity issues | Privacy by Default | LOW | HIGH | Seeds should be dev-only. No visible environment check to prevent production seeding |

## Known Ambiguities

Findings where reasonable reviewers might disagree.

| Finding | Why It's Ambiguous | Acceptable Interpretations |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Framework-handled authentication security (Wasp auth) | Wasp framework abstracts session management, cookie security, CSRF protection | Some reviewers will trust the framework's security defaults; others will flag the lack of explicit configuration as a visibility issue |
| Payment processor data sharing | Stripe/LemonSqueezy/Polar receive email, user ID, payment data by design for payment processing | Legal basis could be "contractual necessity" (GDPR Art. 6.1.b) vs. requiring separate consent; privacy experts disagree |
| Console logging severity | 31 instances of console.log/error found, but context varies (some are debug info, some log user data) | Some reviewers rate this CRITICAL if PII is logged; others rate LOW if it's just system state. Actual severity depends on what's logged |
| Storing emails in User table | Email is both authentication credential and PII - dual purpose complicates minimization analysis | Could argue it's necessary for auth and communication vs. should separate auth (username/ID) from contact info |
| Third-party script loading (Google Analytics, Plausible) | Scripts load from external CDNs, but one claims privacy-friendly (Plausible) | Plausible's privacy claims might justify less scrutiny vs. all third-party scripts should be treated equally |
| Admin dashboard access controls | Admin users can view all user emails, payment IDs, subscription status (UsersTable.tsx) | Role-appropriate access vs. over-privileged admins. Depends on organization size and admin trustworthiness |

## Red Herrings

Things that look like issues within this skill's focus area but are not.

| Item | Why It Looks Like an Issue | Why It's Not |
|------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Dummy email provider in development (main.wasp line 94) | Appears to send emails insecurely | Explicitly marked for development only with warning comment. Production requires real provider |
| Test/sandbox API keys in .env.server.example | Secrets visible in example file | Example file, not actual secrets. Contains placeholder values like "sk_test_..." to guide developers |
| User.isAdmin boolean (schema.prisma line 16) | Seems like crude authorization | This is a template - simple RBAC is appropriate for starter projects. Not a privacy issue, just simple access control |
| Exposing user createdAt timestamp (schema.prisma line 12) | Could reveal user join date to unauthorized parties | Timestamps are normal audit fields. Only privacy concern if exposed to other users, which it isn't (admin-only) |
| File.s3Key stored in database (schema.prisma line 63) | Looks like exposing S3 internals | Necessary reference to retrieve files. Key is randomized UUID (s3Utils.ts line 88), not revealing user data |
| webhook.ts middleware deleting express.json (stripe/webhook.ts line 24, lemonSqueezy/webhook.ts line 92) | Looks like security misconfiguration | Intentional - webhooks need raw body for signature verification. express.raw replaces it |
| Email and username both being the same for email auth (userSignupFields.ts lines 18-21) | Seems like data duplication | Design choice for compatibility with different auth methods. Username can differ for OAuth providers |
| Google Analytics private key in base64 (googleAnalyticsUtils.ts lines 4-7) | Looks like exposed secret | Server-side only, loaded from env var. Base64 is encoding, not encryption, but kept in secure server env |
